title: Solve Problems with Unit Rates
why: To compare deals or scale up, you first find the unit rate (the cost or amount for one) by dividing, then multiply to get more. Warm up dividing-to-find-one and your times facts.
skills: Division facts (find the rate for one)

diagnostic.q: To find the cost of ONE item from a group price, you…

learn.heading: Use the rate for one
learn.intro: First divide to find the amount for one (the unit rate). Then multiply that rate to find the amount for any number you want.
examples: $8 for 4 pens → 8 ÷ 4 = $2 per pen. Then 10 pens = 2 × 10 = $20.
Lower unit rate = better deal: $2/pen beats $3/pen.

tiers.intro: Find the rate for one, then build up.

items.q: A. $6 for 3 pens → cost of 1 pen? (6 ÷ 3)
B. If 1 pen is $2, how much for 2 pens? (2 × 2)
C. How much for 5 pens at $2 each? (2 × 5)

tiers.intro: Find a rate, then scale it.

items.q: A. $20 for 4 tickets → cost of 1 ticket? (20 ÷ 4)
B. At $5 per ticket, how much for 3 tickets? (5 × 3)
C. Which is the BETTER deal?
items.hint: Better deal = lower price for ONE. $2 is the cheapest.

items.q: A. 18 miles in 3 hours → miles per hour, then how far in 5 hours? (18 ÷ 3, then × 5)
B. $12 for 4 yogurts → price each, then cost of 7? (12 ÷ 4, then × 7)

exit.q: 2. At $2 per pen, how much for 6 pens? (2 × 6)
